metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (or sub-bullet in AC4) for `fsck --repair` healing behavior — e.g., 'after repair run, previously absent/corrupt ensures are re-applied and committed exactly once'.
findings.finding: The 'Scope / In' bullet explicitly includes 'server/fsck --repair healing behavior' but no acceptance criterion covers it; the no_mutate AC only covers read-only fsck, leaving the repair/healing path entirely without a verifiable criterion.
findings.location: Scope section ('In:') and AC: no_mutate + absent/garbage-marker
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC5: 'fsck --repair test: running `fsck --repair` on a store with a corrupt/missing .ensure-applied rewrites the file and produces exactly one new commit; a second repair run produces zero new commits (idempotency).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC4 into two separate ACs: one for 'read-only fsck mutates nothing' and one for 'missing/garbage .ensure-applied degrades to empty-set without crashing', so each can be independently verified and checked off.
findings.finding: AC4 bundles two logically independent behaviors — 'read-only fsck mutates nothing' and 'missing/garbage .ensure-applied degrades to empty-set without crashing' — into a single checkbox, so partial completion (one behavior tested, other not) could be checked off.
findings.suggested_fix: AC4a: 'no_mutate: running fsck in read-only mode on a store with fixable issues leaves the filesystem and git history identical to before (diff assertion).' AC4b: 'absent/garbage marker: missing or corrupt .ensure-applied is treated as empty-set; sweep converges without crashing and without a re-commit storm (≤1 new commit).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC for the `fsck --repair` healing behavior (e.g., 'after `--repair` completes, `run_ensures` is called and the store converges with no additional commits on a second run') or move 'server/fsck --repair healing behavior' to Scope Out with a note that it is covered by WS3.
findings.finding: The Scope 'In' section declares 'server/fsck --repair healing behavior' as in-scope, but no AC bullet covers the `--repair` path. AC4 covers only the `no_mutate=True` read-only path (library fsck). The `_repair_run` function exists and is a distinct code path from `no_mutate` scanning.
findings.location: Scope section ('In: ... server/fsck --repair healing behavior'); AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC bullet explicitly covering fsck --repair healing behavior (e.g., 'fsck --repair healing test: running fsck --repair on a store with a corrupt/missing ensure-applied rewrites the marker correctly and converges without data loss'), or remove 'server/fsck --repair healing behavior' from the Scope In list if it is not intended to have its own test here.
findings.finding: The Scope 'In' section declares 'the server/fsck --repair healing behavior' as in-scope, but none of the four AC bullets covers a fsck --repair healing scenario. The no_mutate AC only covers the read-only (non-repair) path. This is an orphan deliverable with no verifying criterion.
findings.location: Scope section ('In:'); AC bullet 4 ('no_mutate + absent/garbage-marker tests')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the self-contradictory naming note in Why: pick one canonical term ('staleness-hint' or 'pending-hint'), correct the parenthetical to read 'this is the X, consistent with WS2 — not Y', and use that term consistently throughout the plan body.
findings.finding: The Why section contains a self-contradictory naming clarification: '(Naming: this is the "pending-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "pending-hint".)' — it asserts the concept both IS and IS NOT named 'pending-hint'. Combined with the plan title's use of 'staleness-hint', there are now three competing names for the same concept with no clear resolution.
findings.location: Why section, naming parenthetical; also plan title ('staleness-hint')
findings.suggested_fix: If the canonical name from WS2 is 'staleness-hint', change the note to: '(Naming: this is the "staleness-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "pending-hint".)' and replace 'pending-hint' in the What section accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly defer with rationale) for the 'staleness-hint' test category named in the title, or rename/remove it from the title to match the actual AC set.
findings.finding: The plan title promises a 'staleness-hint' test category, but no Acceptance Criterion and no body section covers staleness-hint behavior. The body only mentions 'pending-hint' in a confusing self-contradictory naming note, never specifying what a staleness-hint test would assert or validate.
findings.location: Title / What section / Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Either add an AC such as 'Staleness-hint test: when `.ensure-applied` predates the last commit by more than the configured TTL, the hint is treated as stale and the sweep re-evaluates ensures without crashing' — or, if this concept was renamed to pending-hint and is covered by WS2, remove 'staleness-hint' from the WS5 title and add a cross-reference note: 'staleness-hint unit tests live in WS2'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC for the 'staleness-hint' test category (specifying what behavior it asserts) or remove 'staleness-hint' from the plan title and correct the naming parenthetical to pick one canonical term ('staleness-hint' OR 'pending-hint') used consistently.
findings.finding: The plan title names 'staleness-hint' as one of six test categories, but no Acceptance Criterion and no body section specifies what a staleness-hint test asserts. The only naming reference in the body is the self-contradictory parenthetical in Why, which uses 'pending-hint' — neither resolves what staleness-hint behavior should be tested.
findings.location: Plan title; Why section naming parenthetical; Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: If 'staleness-hint' maps to the WS2-owned pending-hint unit tests, remove it from the WS5 title and add: '(staleness-hint unit tests are owned by WS2, not here)' to the Scope/Out section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC covering the staleness-hint/pending-hint behavior (e.g., 'a converged store that gains a new ensure unit emits the hint at most once and never re-emits after convergence') or remove 'staleness-hint' from the title and Why section to prevent a false coverage claim.
findings.finding: The plan title and Why section promise a 'staleness-hint' test category ('idempotency, hot-path regression, staleness-hint, concurrency, no_mutate, absent-marker'), but no Acceptance Criterion and no body text covers staleness-hint behavior. The Why section then re-introduces 'pending-hint' with a confusing self-contradictory naming note, but neither term maps to any AC. The plan does not solve the named problem for this category.
Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific lock mechanism (e.g., `fcntl` exclusive lock on `.ensure-applied.lock`, `flock`, DB advisory lock) in the concurrency AC so the test can assert acquisition/serialization of that primitive.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC requires asserting that 'two processes serialize under the write lock' but the lock mechanism (file lock, fcntl advisory lock, OS-level rename, etc.) is never named, so an executor cannot write an assertion about lock acquisition or verify the right primitive is used.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the concurrency AC: '…serialize under an `fcntl`/`flock` exclusive lock on `.ensure-applied.lock` (or whichever primitive WS1 specifies); the test asserts the second process blocks until the first releases the lock.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the concurrency AC to require the test to spawn real OS subprocesses (e.g. `multiprocessing` or subprocess invocation) so that OS-level file-lock serialization is actually exercised, not bypassed by an in-process threading substitute.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC requires 'two processes sweeping the same checkout serialize under the write lock', but the plan does not specify that the test must use real OS-level subprocesses. If implemented with threads and an in-process lock, the test bypasses the actual file-locking boundary (the OS advisory/exclusive lock), satisfies all written ACs, and goes green even if the real locking path is broken — a proxy-validation anti-pattern on the riskiest AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test AC: 'two processes sweeping the same checkout serialize under the write lock and converge; `.ensure-applied` is atomic (no torn/lost writes)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the concurrency AC: 'The test MUST use real OS subprocesses (e.g. via `multiprocessing.Process` or `subprocess.Popen`) — in-process threads do not satisfy this criterion because they bypass OS advisory file-lock semantics.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend AC3 to explicitly require real OS-level subprocesses (e.g., `subprocess.run` or `multiprocessing`) for the concurrency test, matching the pattern in `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py`, so fcntl inter-process serialization is actually exercised.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC requires two processes to serialize under the write lock, but the plan never mandates that the test use real OS-level subprocesses. fcntl.flock (the actual lock mechanism in lock.py) is process-granular: a single process can re-acquire its own flock without blocking, so a thread-based implementation of this test would always pass regardless of whether the inter-process serialization invariant holds. An implementer following only the AC text could use threads or in-process calls and produce a false-green test that exercises no actual lock contention.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'the test must spawn real OS-level subprocesses (subprocess.run / multiprocessing.Process), not threads, because fcntl.flock is process-granular and a thread-based test would not exercise inter-process serialization.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the concurrency test uses real OS processes (subprocess.Popen or equivalent, NOT threading) to exercise the fcntl.flock/mkdir dual-window lock, and explicitly reference the established test_snapshot_cache.py::test_cross_process_single_flight pattern as the model to follow.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC specifies 'two processes sweeping the same checkout serialize under the write lock' but does not reference or align with the established cross-process test pattern already present in the codebase (test_snapshot_cache.py::test_cross_process_single_flight, which uses subprocess.Popen + a shared marker/barrier file + p.wait()). Leaving the mechanism unspecified risks reinventing a different, potentially incompatible approach (e.g., threading instead of real OS processes, or a multiprocessing.Process that doesn't exercise the fcntl.flock/mkdir dual-window lock correctly).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Concurrency AC: 'Implemented via subprocess.Popen (real OS processes, not threads) following the pattern in tests/unit/test_snapshot_cache.py::test_cross_process_single_flight — each child process is a -c script that imports and calls run_ensures(), with a shared marker file or lock-release signal for synchronization; p.wait(timeout=N) asserts no hang.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the actual fsck byte-parity test file path (the docstring in `fsck.py` names `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` but it does not exist) and update the WS3 scope-exclusion to reference the real file(s) so the exclusion doesn't silently drop work.
findings.finding: The scope-exclusion 'fsck byte-parity/help-golden tests are owned by WS3' is a codebase-checkable claim: `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` line 15 names `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` as the byte-parity pin file, but that file does not exist anywhere under `tests/`. The actual fsck test coverage lives in `tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_push_pending.py`, `test_fsck_snapshot_checks.py`, etc. If WS3 must update a named file that doesn't exist, the exclusion silently drops work.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'fsck byte-parity/help goldens (WS3)' with the actual file paths (`tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_push_pending.py`, `test_fsck_snapshot_checks.py`) and note that `test_e4_fsck.py` is a stale docstring reference, not a live file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate at least two concrete 'garbage' cases for the `.ensure-applied` test fixture (e.g., empty file, invalid UTF-8 bytes, valid JSON with wrong schema) so the AC is unambiguously testable.
findings.finding: 'Garbage `.ensure-applied`' is undefined — an executor cannot write a fixture without knowing what inputs constitute 'garbage' (empty file, invalid UTF-8, valid JSON with wrong schema, truncated mid-write temp file, etc.).
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4 to: '…missing/garbage .ensure-applied (at minimum: zero-byte file, invalid UTF-8, well-formed JSON with unexpected schema) degrades to empty-set without crashing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a single canonical proving command (e.g., `pytest tests/behavioral/test_ws5.py -v` or equivalent) that, when green, proves all four ACs are satisfied.
findings.finding: No proving command (e.g., `pytest tests/behavioral/ -v`, a specific test module path, or CI job name) is provided for any AC, making it impossible to verify done-ness mechanically without hunting through the repo.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'How to verify' section: `pytest tests/behavioral/test_ws5.py -v  # must show 4 passing tests covering idempotency, hot-path, concurrency, no_mutate+absent`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test-isolation strategy to the plan (e.g., each test case operates on a fresh `tempdir` clone, or fixtures explicitly create/delete `.ensure-applied` in setup/teardown) so the CREATE and RETIRE lifecycle of `.ensure-applied` in the test environment is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan does not define test isolation for `.ensure-applied` across test cases. Tests (idempotency, concurrency, absent/garbage-marker) each require distinct pre-conditions for this shared file (populated, absent, garbage). Without explicit teardown or per-test temporary directories, state from one test can leak into another, causing false positives on the idempotency test or masking the absent-marker degradation path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all four bullet points; 'Shared-state lifecycle' for `.ensure-applied`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Test Setup' section specifying: each test case provisions a fresh temporary git checkout (via `tempfile`/`pytest tmp_path` or equivalent); `.ensure-applied` state is seeded explicitly per test (populated/absent/garbage); teardown deletes the temp directory unconditionally. Reference this in each AC bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the concurrency AC how atomicity of `.ensure-applied` writes is verified (e.g., a third observer process reads the file repeatedly during the two-process sweep and asserts it never observes a partial/invalid state, OR the test asserts the implementation uses an atomic `rename`/`fsync` pattern via code inspection or a recorded syscall trace).
findings.finding: The concurrency AC states `.ensure-applied` must be 'atomic (no torn/lost writes)' but does not specify how the test verifies this property. Without a concrete assertion mechanism (e.g., injected fault between open and rename, a read during write, or a post-hoc hash check), the test may only verify that two processes converge to the same content — which does not rule out a torn intermediate state that happened to be overwritten.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test bullet: 'two processes sweeping the same checkout serialize under the write lock and converge; `.ensure-applied` is atomic (no torn/lost writes)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a forward-reference to the WS1 `run_ensures` interface contract (module path, signature, and the subprocess-suppression invariant it must uphold) so AC2's hot-path regression test has a grounded anchor to instrument rather than asserting against a not-yet-existing symbol.
findings.finding: `run_ensures()` does not exist anywhere in `src/rebar/` — it is the WS1 deliverable. The hot-path AC (AC2) makes three confident behavioral assertions about it ('never calls `run_ensures`', 'spawns no git-config subprocess', 'reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process') without citing any interface contract or stub target, leaving test authors with no grounded anchor for what to instrument or mock.
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC2 row, cite the WS1 deliverable location (e.g., `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py::run_ensures`) and state which layer owns the 'no git-config subprocess on converged store' invariant (e.g., `_ensure_branch_user_config`'s guard condition), so the test knows exactly what call path to trace and what to stub.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name at least one concrete test-file path per AC cluster (e.g., `tests/interfaces/store/test_run_ensures_idempotency.py` for ACs 1–2, `tests/integration/test_run_ensures_concurrency.py` for AC 3, etc.) so edit targets are verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan names no concrete test-file paths (e.g., `tests/interfaces/store/test_ensures_idempotency.py`). Every edit target for this workstream is unspecified, making it impossible to verify existence or enumerate consumers/callers that import the new test helpers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale to the Why or What section explaining why real-subprocess behavioral tests (not in-process unit tests with mocks) are required here — specifically that fcntl-based lock correctness can only be verified across OS process boundaries.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for WHY behavioral/subprocess-level testing is the right approach for these invariants. The Why section justifies having tests at all ('only tests lock the invariants') but says nothing about why real-subprocess behavioral tests (vs. unit tests with mocks, or CLI end-to-end tests) are the correct layer. This absence matters especially for the concurrency test, where the right choice (real subprocesses vs. threads) is non-obvious and load-bearing.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
